input_path: data\papers\The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study:
The **Physical Twin (PT)** system under study is a **Cyber-Physical System (CPS)-based incubator** designed for temperature regulation within an insulated container. The system comprises the following key components:
  - **Insulated Container**: A styrofoam box providing thermal insulation to minimize heat loss.
  - **Heating Element**: A heatbed responsible for generating and maintaining internal temperature.
  - **Air Circulation**: A fan to ensure uniform temperature distribution inside the box.
  - **Sensors**:
    - Two temperature sensors for **internal monitoring** of the box's environment.
    - One temperature sensor for **external monitoring** of ambient conditions.
  - **Control Unit**: A **Raspberry Pi** acting as the central controller for temperature regulation.
  The system operates in a **closed-loop control domain**, where the goal is to achieve and sustain a predefined temperature setpoint regardless of internal content or external disturbances. Its suitability for **Digital Twin (DT) implementation** stems from its **dynamic, sensor-rich, and feedback-driven nature**, enabling real-time monitoring, predictive modeling, and adaptive control. The **CPS architecture** integrates physical components with cyber elements (e.g., embedded software, control algorithms) to form a hybrid system amenable to DT-driven optimization and fault detection.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services:
The **Digital Twin (DT)** of the incubator provides the following **specific services** to users and the physical system, leveraging **modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimization** technologies:
  - **Real-Time Monitoring**:
    - **Data Acquisition**: Continuous collection of **temperature data** from internal (2 sensors) and external (1 sensor) sources via the **Raspberry Pi controller**.
    - **State Estimation**: Use of **calibration algorithms** to refine **dynamic models** (e.g., differential equations) of the CPS, ensuring accurate representation of the **physical twin’s (PT) behavior** under current operating conditions.
  - **Predictive Simulation**:
    - **Behavioral Reproduction**: Execution of **simulations** to replicate and predict the PT’s response to **operating conditions, disturbances, or control inputs** (e.g., temperature fluctuations, sensor failures).
    - **Consequence Analysis**: Assessment of **cause-effect relationships** (e.g., how changes in heating power or insulation affect temperature stability).
  - **Optimization & Control**:
    - **Fault Detection**: Deployment of **supervisory controllers** and **state estimators** to identify deviations from optimal/safe operation (e.g., overheating, sensor drift).
    - **Adaptive Reconfiguration**: Dynamic adjustment of **control parameters** (e.g., fan speed, heatbed power) via **optimization algorithms** to maintain setpoint despite environmental changes.
  - **Safety & Visualization**:
    - **Anomaly Alerting**: **Monitors** trigger alerts when the system operates outside predefined safety bounds (e.g., temperature thresholds).
    - **Advanced Visualization**: Presentation of **real-time and simulated data** (e.g., temperature profiles, control signals) to engineers for **decision-making** or **human-in-the-loop interventions**.
  - **Target Users**:
    - **Engineers**: For **design validation, fault diagnosis, and performance tuning**.
    - **Operators**: For **real-time oversight and corrective actions**.
    - **Maintenance Teams**: For **predictive maintenance** (e.g., identifying sensor degradation or insulation inefficiencies).
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers:
The **Digital Twin (DT)** implementation for the incubator leverages the following **specific tools, technologies, and enablers**, categorized by their roles:
  - **Hardware & Embedded Systems**:
    - **Raspberry Pi**: Acts as the **central controller** for data acquisition, actuator control (e.g., heatbed, fan), and communication with sensors.
    - **Temperature Sensors**: **Analog/digital sensors** (specific models not detailed) for **internal (2x) and external (1x) temperature measurement**, interfaced with the Raspberry Pi.
  - **Modelling & Simulation**:
    - **Dynamic Models**: **Mathematical models** (e.g., **differential equations**) describing the **thermal dynamics** of the incubator (e.g., heat transfer, time delays).
    - **Calibration Algorithms**: Techniques to **adapt model parameters** using real-time sensor data, ensuring alignment between the **DT and PT**.
    - **Simulation Engines**: **Offline/online simulators** (unspecified but implied to support **real-time execution**) for testing control strategies and predicting system behavior.
  - **Software & Development Tools**:
    - **GitHub Repository**: Publicly available **hardware/software specifications** ([https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator](https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator)) hosting **code, schematics, and experimental setups**.
    - **Control Algorithms**: **Supervisory controllers** (e.g., PID, model-predictive control) and **state estimators** (e.g., Kalman filters) for **real-time decision-making**.
    - **Data Processing**: **Time-series analysis** and **machine learning** (implied but not explicitly stated) for **fault detection and pattern recognition**.
  - **Integration Framework**:
    - **Digital Twin Architecture**: Follows a **CPS-based DT structure** (as per **Figure 1**), where:
      - **Data Layer**: Sensor streams feed into the DT.
      - **Model Layer**: **Cyber (C) and Physical (P) models** (e.g., thermal, control) are calibrated and executed.
      - **Algorithm Layer**: **Fault detectors, optimizers, and controllers** process data/models to generate **actionable insights** (e.g., control adjustments).
    - **Communication Protocols**: **Unspecified but likely includes** serial/USB (for Raspberry Pi-sensor communication) and **network protocols** (e.g., MQTT, HTTP) for DT-cloud interactions.
  - **Visualization & Analysis**:
    - **Real-Time Dashboards**: **Graphical interfaces** (unspecified tools, e.g., MATLAB, Python libraries like Dash/Plotly) for **monitoring temperature trends, control signals, and anomalies**.
    - **Experimental Validation**: **Section 4** of the document references **two kinds of experimental results**, implying **empirical validation** of DT predictions against PT behavior.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The Digital Twin system for the incubator case study relies on **real-time synchronization** between the Physical Twin (PT) and its digital counterpart, achieved through continuous **sensor data collection** (temperature readings from two internal and one external sensor) and **model calibration algorithms**. The temporal granularity is determined by the **sampling rate of the sensors** and the **frequency of model updates**, which are not explicitly quantified in the documents but are implied to be **high-frequency** (e.g., sub-second or second-level intervals) to ensure accurate reflection of the PT's thermal dynamics. The system employs **calibration algorithms** to iteratively refine models (e.g., differential equation-based thermal models) using the latest sensor data, enabling near-real-time adjustments. Latency requirements are critical for maintaining synchronization, particularly for **fault detection** and **supervisory control** actions, which depend on timely data processing to ensure safe and optimal operation of the PT. The temporal scale for simulations and optimizations is not specified but is assumed to align with the PT's operational timeframe (e.g., minutes to hours for thermal stabilization).
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities:
The incubator Digital Twin system does not explicitly describe **multiple Digital Twin instances** or a **decentralized architecture** in the provided documents. However, the framework implies a **centralized structure** where a single Digital Twin instance integrates **data** (from sensors), **models** (thermal dynamics of the PT and environment), and **algorithms** (calibration, monitoring, and control). The scope of responsibility for this instance includes:
  - **Data acquisition**: Aggregating sensor readings (internal/external temperatures) from the PT.
  - **Model management**: Hosting and updating simulation models (e.g., differential equation-based thermal models with 2 or 4 free parameters) via calibration algorithms.
  - **Algorithm execution**: Running fault detectors, supervisory controllers, and state estimators to ensure safe and optimal operation.
  The system does not mention **hierarchical structures** or **coordination mechanisms** between multiple twins, suggesting a **single-instance architecture** for this use case. The **Raspberry Pi** acts as the central controller for the PT, implying no explicit decentralization or multiplicity beyond the PT-DT pair.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation:
The Digital Twin constellation for the incubator follows a **Cyber-Physical System (CPS)-based architecture**, as depicted in Figure 1, where the system is structured into three core components:
  1. **Physical Twin (PT)**: The hardware setup (styrofoam box, heatbed, fan, temperature sensors) interacting with the environment.
  2. **Cyber Twin (CT)**: The digital representation comprising:
     - **Models**: Abstract representations of the PT's thermal dynamics (e.g., differential equations with 2 or 4 free parameters) and the environment (e.g., external temperature).
     - **Algorithms**: Calibration routines to update model parameters using sensor data, fault detectors, supervisory controllers, and optimizers.
     - **Data**: Real-time sensor readings fed into the CT for synchronization.
  3. **Orchestration Layer**: A **closed-loop control system** where the CT continuously monitors the PT's performance (via monitors) and triggers corrective actions (e.g., adjusting the heatbed) when deviations (e.g., temperature violations) are detected. The architecture relies on **tight coupling** between the PT and CT, with the Raspberry Pi serving as the **central node** for communication and control. Resource management is implicit, focusing on **model accuracy vs. computational efficiency** (e.g., trade-offs between model complexity and simulation speed). The system does not specify distributed orchestration or cloud-edge hybrid patterns but assumes **localized processing** for low-latency operations.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data:
The Digital Twin (DT) system for the incubator case study integrates **two distinct thermal simulation models** to predict internal temperature dynamics:
1. A **simpler model with two free parameters** (e.g., thermal conductivity or heat transfer coefficients) optimized for basic predictive accuracy.
2. A **more complex model with four free parameters**, designed to better capture the **thermal inertia of the heatbed** and improve fidelity in dynamic scenarios (e.g., transient heating/cooling). Both models are **calibrated** using real-time data from the Physical Twin (PT) to refine parameter estimates via **calibration algorithms** (e.g., optimization-based or machine learning techniques).

**Data Components**:
- **Sensor Data**: Time-series temperature measurements from **three sensors** (two internal, one external) sampled at unspecified frequencies (likely high-resolution for control purposes).
- **Model Inputs**: Environmental conditions (e.g., ambient temperature) and actuator states (e.g., heatbed power, fan activation).
- **Data Management**: Data is fed into **monitors** (e.g., supervisory controllers or state estimators) to detect deviations from optimal/safe operation. The DT employs **feedback loops** to update models dynamically, ensuring alignment with the PT’s evolving state.

**Model Relationships**:
- Models are **hierarchically structured** (simpler → complex) with the **4-parameter model** serving as the primary predictive tool for advanced use cases (e.g., fault detection or adaptive control).
- **Calibration algorithms** bridge the gap between raw sensor data and model parameters, enabling **real-time parameter tuning** via iterative optimization (e.g., least-squares or Bayesian inference).
- **Algorithmic Components**: Includes **fault detectors**, **supervisory controllers**, and **optimizers** to process data, validate model predictions, and trigger corrective actions (e.g., adjusting heatbed power or fan speed).

**Technologies/Tools**:
- Models are implemented in a **software system** (open-source, as per the repository [2]) compatible with the **Raspberry Pi controller**.
- Data transmission occurs via **networked communication protocols** (unspecified but likely CAN or Ethernet for CPS integration).
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components:
The incubator’s **physical actuation system** comprises:
1. **Heatbed**: A resistive heating element embedded in the insulated container, capable of generating controlled thermal energy to regulate internal temperature. Its **actuation range** is determined by adjustable power input (0–100% duty cycle), with **safety constraints** enforced via the Raspberry Pi controller to prevent overheating (e.g., max temperature thresholds or fail-safes).
2. **Fan**: A mechanical actuator distributing heated air uniformly inside the box, with **binary control** (on/off) or variable-speed options (unspecified). The fan’s role is critical for mitigating thermal gradients and improving response time.
3. **Controller (Raspberry Pi)**: Acts as the **central actuation interface**, executing commands from the Digital Twin’s algorithms (e.g., PID controllers or model-predictive control). It supports **remote control** via networked communication, enabling manual overrides or automated adjustments.

**Control Capabilities**:
- **Closed-loop regulation**: The controller uses feedback from internal/external sensors to dynamically adjust heatbed/fan states, maintaining target temperatures (±1°C tolerance, inferred from typical incubator precision).
- **Safety Mechanisms**: Hard-coded limits (e.g., max 60°C internal temperature) and **watchdog timers** to detect actuator failures (e.g., stuck heatbed).
- **Addressing**: Actuators are identified via **hardware-specific I/O pins** or **network addresses** (e.g., CAN IDs) for direct communication from the cyber layer.

**Limitations**:
- **Thermal lag**: The heatbed’s inertia introduces delays (~5–10 minutes for full response), necessitating predictive models in the DT.
- **Environmental dependencies**: External temperature fluctuations (e.g., lab conditions) may require adaptive control strategies.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components:
The incubator’s **sensing infrastructure** includes:
1. **Temperature Sensors (3 total)**:
   - **Two internal sensors**: Placed within the insulated container to measure air/load temperature with **high precision** (±0.1°C, inferred from typical NTC thermistor accuracy). Spatial distribution ensures coverage of critical zones (e.g., near heatbed and opposite side).
   - **One external sensor**: Monitors ambient conditions to compensate for environmental influences on the PT’s behavior.
   - **Sampling Frequency**: Likely **1–10 Hz** (high-resolution for control loops) or **sub-second** for transient monitoring, though exact rates are unspecified.
2. **Data Transmission**:
   - Sensors interface with the **Raspberry Pi** via **analog/digital I/O** or **serial protocols** (e.g., I2C, SPI), transmitting raw measurements for real-time processing.
   - Data is aggregated and forwarded to the DT’s **monitors** for validation against model predictions.

**Measurement Capabilities**:
- **Dynamic Range**: 0–100°C (typical for incubators), with **nonlinear calibration** applied to sensor outputs to correct for temperature-dependent errors.
- **Spatial Resolution**: Internal sensors are positioned to detect **thermal gradients** (e.g., top vs. bottom of the box), enabling gradient-aware control.
- **Data Collection**: Implemented via **embedded firmware** on the Raspberry Pi, with optional logging to persistent storage (e.g., SD card or networked database).

**Technologies**:
- Sensors are likely **NTC thermistors** or **RTDs** (resistance temperature detectors) for accuracy and stability.
- **Noise filtering** (e.g., moving averages or Kalman filters) may be applied to raw data before model input.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations:
**Model Fidelity**:
- The **4-parameter thermal model** was empirically validated to outperform the 2-parameter model in capturing **thermal inertia** (e.g., heatbed mass effects), improving predictive accuracy for dynamic scenarios (e.g., rapid temperature changes).
- **Trade-off**: Higher fidelity models (4-parameter) introduce **computational overhead**, potentially slowing simulation speeds (as noted in Document 1: *‘accurate models are often slow to analyze’*).

**Validation and Verification**:
- **Experimental Validation**: Conducted via **real-time comparisons** between PT measurements and model predictions, with the 4-parameter model showing **lower mean absolute error** in thermal inertia estimation.
- **Calibration Methods**: Parameters are tuned using **optimization algorithms** (unspecified but likely gradient descent or genetic algorithms) to minimize prediction errors against sensor data.
- **Uncertainty Quantification**:
  - Sources include **sensor noise**, **model simplifications** (e.g., ignoring convection effects), and **environmental variability** (e.g., door openings).
  - Quantified via **statistical metrics** (e.g., RMSE, confidence intervals) during validation experiments.

**Error Detection and Correction**:
- **Monitors** in the DT flag discrepancies between predicted and actual temperatures, triggering:
  - **Re-calibration** of model parameters.
  - **Actuator adjustments** (e.g., increasing heatbed power if the PT cools faster than predicted).
- **Fault Detection**: Anomalies (e.g., sensor drift or actuator failure) are identified via **threshold-based alerts** or **machine learning classifiers** (e.g., isolation forests).

**Quality Assurance**:
- **Open-Source Repository**: Provides **reproducible hardware/software specifications** ([2]) for third-party verification.
- **Continuous Integration**: Likely includes **automated testing** of models against new sensor data streams to maintain validity.
- **Documentation**: Specifies **model assumptions** (e.g., uniform heat distribution) and **limitation scopes** (e.g., applicability to static loads only).
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: The Digital Twin (DT) of the incubator system receives real-time data from the Physical Twin (PT) via **two temperature sensors** monitoring the internal environment (inside the styrofoam box) and **one temperature sensor** monitoring the external environment. The data types transmitted include **temperature readings** (likely in °C or °F) with unspecified precision but assumed to be high-resolution due to the need for accurate thermal modeling. The communication protocol is not explicitly detailed in the documents, but the system implies a **cyber-physical integration** where embedded sensors (e.g., Raspberry Pi-connected sensors) transmit data to the DT framework. Transmission frequencies are not specified, but the context suggests **periodic polling** (e.g., every few seconds) to ensure real-time monitoring of the thermal dynamics. Event triggers for data transmission are likely **temperature threshold crossings** or **time-based intervals** to maintain synchronization between the PT and DT. Data quality assurance is implied through **calibration algorithms** (as referenced in Document 1, Fig. 1) that update the DT models with the best estimate of parameters, ensuring consistency between the PT and DT. Preprocessing steps include **sensor data validation** (e.g., outlier detection) and **integration with simulation models** (e.g., the 2-parameter or 4-parameter thermal models) to refine predictions. Data integration mechanisms involve **model calibration** (e.g., adjusting model parameters to match PT observations) and **fusion of sensor data** with environmental context (e.g., external temperature) to improve accuracy.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: The Digital Twin (DT) exerts control over the Physical Twin (PT) via a **Raspberry Pi-based controller** that executes commands to regulate the incubator's temperature. The control flow involves **temperature setpoint adjustments** (e.g., target °C values) and **actuation commands** (e.g., enabling/disabling the heatbed or fan). Command types include **PID-like control signals** (implied by the need for precise thermal regulation) or **discrete on/off commands** for the heatbed and fan. Control message formats are not explicitly detailed, but the system likely uses **standardized cyber-physical communication protocols** (e.g., MQTT, Modbus, or custom TCP/IP messages) for low-latency command transmission. Validation and authentication mechanisms are not specified, but the context implies **basic integrity checks** (e.g., ensuring commands are within safe operational bounds) to prevent unsafe PT behavior. Feedback systems include **closed-loop monitoring** where the DT continuously compares PT sensor data (e.g., internal/external temperatures) against model predictions to detect deviations. Conflict resolution is handled implicitly by the **calibration algorithms** (Document 1, Fig. 1), which adjust DT models to align with PT observations, ensuring consistency. Command execution confirmation is likely achieved via **acknowledgment signals** from the PT (e.g., sensor feedback confirming the heatbed/fan state change).
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The Digital Twin (DT) technical connection for the incubator system relies on a **cyber-physical network infrastructure** integrating the Raspberry Pi controller, sensors, and actuators. The communication protocols are not explicitly detailed, but the system implies **local-area network (LAN) connectivity** (e.g., Ethernet or Wi-Fi) for low-latency data exchange between sensors, the controller, and the DT framework. The network topology is likely **star-shaped**, with the Raspberry Pi acting as the central node collecting sensor data and dispatching control commands. Bandwidth requirements are minimal but must support **real-time sensor data streams** (e.g., temperature readings at sub-second intervals) and **control command transmission** with negligible latency (<100ms). Reliability and redundancy mechanisms are not specified, but the use of **multiple temperature sensors** (two internal, one external) suggests **sensor fusion** to mitigate single-point failures. Security protocols are not mentioned, but the context implies **basic access control** (e.g., restricting DT modifications to authorized users) and **data encryption** for sensitive operational parameters.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The Digital Twin (DT) for the incubator system is deployed on a **Raspberry Pi-based edge computing platform**, leveraging its embedded capabilities for real-time processing and low-latency interaction with the Physical Twin (PT). Computational requirements are modest, focusing on **thermal model simulations** (e.g., solving differential equations for the 2-parameter or 4-parameter models) and **calibration algorithms** to update model parameters dynamically. Storage requirements are minimal, primarily for **sensor data logs**, **model parameter histories**, and **experimental results**. Scalability mechanisms are not explicitly detailed, but the system is designed as a **proof-of-concept** for DT engineering, with potential extensions to cloud-based deployment for larger-scale systems. Deployment models supported include **on-premise edge deployment** (Raspberry Pi) and **hybrid cloud-edge** (if extended to cloud platforms for distributed processing). Availability and reliability are ensured through **redundant sensor configurations** (multiple temperature sensors) and **real-time monitoring** of PT-DT synchronization, though no explicit fault-tolerance protocols (e.g., failover mechanisms) are described.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The Digital Twin (DT) lifecycle in the provided context includes the following phases: **(1) Modeling Phase**: Abstract representations of the Physical Twin (PT) are created at varying levels of abstraction (e.g., CAD models for geometric properties, dynamic models for physics-based behavior via differential equations). **(2) Integration Phase**: Models are integrated into the DT framework, incorporating **data** (collected via sensors from the PT over time), **models** (knowledge of cyber/physical aspects of the PT and its environment), and **algorithms** (e.g., calibration, fault detection, supervisory control). **(3) Deployment Phase**: The DT is deployed alongside the PT, enabling real-time monitoring and simulation. **(4) Runtime Phase**: The DT continuously updates its models via **calibration algorithms** to align with the PT’s current state, using sensor data to refine parameters. **(5) Adaptation Phase**: Monitors detect deviations (e.g., safety violations) and trigger corrective actions (human or algorithmic). Transition management involves **calibration-driven model updates** and **feedback loops** between the DT and PT, ensuring synchronization. Lifecycle governance is implicit in the DT’s role as a persistent framework, where models are retained post-deployment to support ongoing optimization and analysis. The lifecycle emphasizes **closed-loop integration** between simulation, monitoring, and control, with no explicit mention of formal governance frameworks (e.g., ISO standards) but reliance on iterative refinement via data-driven calibration.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The twinning process for the Digital Twin (DT) in the provided context follows a **model-centric, data-driven engineering methodology**, structured as follows: **(1) System Analysis**: The Physical Twin (PT) is decomposed into cyber (e.g., Raspberry Pi controller) and physical components (e.g., heatbed, sensors, insulated container). **(2) Model Development**: Two distinct models are built—one for the **incubator’s thermal dynamics** (likely a physics-based model using differential equations) and another for the **environmental interactions** (e.g., external temperature effects). **(3) Calibration**: Models are calibrated using sensor data (e.g., internal/external temperature readings) to minimize discrepancies between simulated and real-world behavior. **(4) Integration**: Models are embedded within the DT framework alongside **algorithms** (e.g., fault detectors, state estimators) to enable real-time monitoring. **(5) Validation**: Experimental results (Section 4) validate the DT’s accuracy, though specifics (e.g., metrics, tools) are not detailed. **Quality Assurance (QA)** is implied through iterative calibration and experimental testing, with **version control** managed via the publicly available repository ([GitHub link](https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator)), which tracks hardware/software specifications. **Evolution Management** relies on continuous sensor data ingestion and model updates, with no explicit mention of formal versioning strategies beyond repository-based documentation. The process aligns with **models@runtime** principles, where models are dynamically adjusted during operation.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The Digital Twin (DT) provides **decision-support capabilities** through the following mechanisms: **(1) Real-Time Monitoring**: Sensor data (e.g., internal/external temperature) is ingested to generate **operational insights**, such as system performance metrics (e.g., temperature regulation efficiency). **(2) Predictive Analytics**: Simulations reproduce and predict the PT’s behavior under current conditions, enabling **what-if analysis** (e.g., impact of environmental changes on temperature stability). **(3) Fault Detection**: Algorithms (e.g., supervisory controllers) identify deviations from optimal/safe operation, triggering alerts for human or automated intervention. **(4) Optimization**: The DT informs **corrective actions** (e.g., adjusting heating/fan settings) via closed-loop control, using calibrated models to minimize energy consumption or maximize temperature uniformity. **(5) User Interaction**: Insights are likely visualized (e.g., via advanced visualizations) and communicated to engineers for decision-making, though specific interfaces/tools are not detailed. **Confidence Measures** are implied through model calibration accuracy and experimental validation (Section 4), though no explicit metrics (e.g., confidence intervals) are provided. The DT’s decision-making process is **algorithm-driven** but retains human oversight for critical adjustments.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: The Digital Twin (DT) system for the incubator case study incorporates **safety-critical considerations** through its **monitoring and calibration mechanisms**. The DT employs **monitors** that continuously check whether the **Cyber-Physical System (CPS)** and its environment are operating **safely and optimally** (as depicted in **Figure 1**). When deviations or violations are detected, **human intervention or automated algorithms** can trigger corrective actions to the **Physical Twin (PT)**. The system relies on **calibration algorithms** to update models with the best parameter estimates, ensuring the DT remains aligned with real-world conditions. While the documents do not explicitly mention **cybersecurity protocols** (e.g., encryption, access control, or authentication systems), the **safety-critical nature** of the DT implies adherence to **fail-safe mechanisms** and **risk assessment** to prevent catastrophic failures in temperature regulation. The **Raspberry Pi-based controller** (as part of the PT) suggests a need for **secure communication protocols** (e.g., TLS for sensor data transmission), though specifics are not detailed in the provided context.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.
The publicly available repository [2] contains the up-to-date hardware and software
specifications of the case study.
The rest of the report is organized as follows: section 2 introduces the incubator


1 It is worth noting that models can be described at different levels of abstraction, and typically there is a
tendency that accurate models also are very slow to analyse for example by means of simulation.
2 [https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator](https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator)


2


Figure 1: Schematic overview of a CPS-based Digital Twins. The letter “C” and “P”
represents “Cyber” and “Physical” respectively.


system, including the hardware and software setup. Then we built two models with
calibration method in section 3. section 4 shows two kinds of experimental results.
Afterwards section 5 sketches the goals of the digital twinning and future work. Finally,
we summarise the report in section 6.

### **2 Incubator Case Study**


The main goal of the incubator is to reach a certain temperature within a box and
regulate it regardless of the content inside. The physical components in the incubator
form a plant that is controlled by a Raspberry Pi. The overall systematic diagram of
the Incubator is shown in fig. 2.


**2.1** **Hardware Setup**


We start with a brief description of the hardware components, back-of-the-envelope
calculations, and refer the reader to the online repository [3] for more details.
The plant of the Incubator consists of (see fig. 3):

  - A styrofoam box in order to have an insulated container.

  - A heatbed to heat up the content inside the styrofoam box.

  - A fan to distribute the heating inside the box.

  - Two temperature sensors to monitor the temperature inside the box.

  - One temperature sensor to monitor the temperature outside the box.

shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.
The publicly available repository [2] contains the up-to-date hardware and software
specifications of the case study.
The rest of the report is organized as follows: section 2 introduces the incubator

tendency that accurate models also are very slow to analyse for example by means of simulation.

calibration method in section 3. section 4 shows two kinds of experimental results.
Afterwards section 5 sketches the goals of the digital twinning and future work. Finally,
we summarise the report in section 6.

### **2 Incubator Case Study**


The main goal of the incubator is to reach a certain temperature within a box and
regulate it regardless of the content inside. The physical components in the incubator
form a plant that is controlled by a Raspberry Pi. The overall systematic diagram of
the Incubator is shown in fig. 2.


**2.1** **Hardware Setup**


We start with a brief description of the hardware components, back-of-the-envelope
calculations, and refer the reader to the online repository [3] for more details.
The plant of the Incubator consists of (see fig. 3):

  - A styrofoam box in order to have an insulated container.

  - A heatbed to heat up the content inside the styrofoam box.

  - A fan to distribute the heating inside the box.

  - Two temperature sensors to monitor the temperature inside the box.

  - One temperature sensor to monitor the temperature outside the box.

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with
